file_name: PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. - A_R STRATEGIC LICENSING, DISTRIBUTION AND MARKETING AGREEMENT .txt

perturbation.original_text: EKR shall obtain and maintain all necessary licenses, permits, records and authorizations required by Applicable Laws as holder of the Transferred NDA after the Agreement Date and in order to exercise the Distribution Rights and observe and comply with all Applicable Laws, ordinances, rules and regulations including, but not limited to those of the applicable Regulatory Authorities in the exercise of the Distribution Rights save insofar as PPI is required to obtain the same as holder of the Marketing Authorizations prior to the Agreement Date, or under the terms of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: EKR shall endeavor to obtain necessary licenses, permits, records and authorizations related to Applicable Laws as holder of the Transferred NDA after the Agreement Date and in order to exercise the Distribution Rights and comply with some Applicable Laws, ordinances, rules and regulations including, but not limited to those of the applicable Regulatory Authorities in the exercise of the Distribution Rights save insofar as PPI is required to obtain the same as holder of the Marketing Authorizations prior to the Agreement Date, or under the terms of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens EKR's obligation to comply with all applicable laws and regulations by replacing 'shall obtain and maintain all necessary' with 'shall endeavor to obtain necessary' and 'observe and comply with all Applicable Laws' with 'comply with some Applicable Laws'. This creates an ambiguous obligation as to which laws EKR must actually comply with.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 211 outlines Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations for finished pharmaceuticals. By stating EKR only needs to comply with 'some' Applicable Laws, the modified text contradicts the CGMP requirement that all aspects of pharmaceutical manufacturing and distribution must adhere to strict regulatory standards, as outlined in 21 CFR § 211.22, which concerns responsibilities of the quality control unit. The FDA requires strict adherence to CGMP to ensure drug safety and efficacy.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens EKR's obligation to comply with applicable laws, stating they only need to comply with 'some' laws instead of 'all necessary' laws. The Law Explanation states this contradicts 21 CFR Part 211 which outlines Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations and requires strict adherence to regulatory standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 211.22 concerning the responsibilities of quality control units, reinforcing the CGMP requirements. By limiting compliance to 'some' laws, the modified contract introduces an ambiguity that undermines the strict regulatory adherence required by CGMP, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall consult with each other, in advance, with regard to the terms of all proposed press releases, public announcements and other public statements with respect to the transactions contemplated under this Agreement. The Parties acknowledge that they have issued a joint press release in the form set out in Schedule VI of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may consider consulting with each other, when practical, with regard to the terms of some proposed press releases, public announcements and other public statements with respect to the transactions contemplated under this Agreement. The Parties acknowledge that they may have issued a joint press release in a form similar to that set out in Schedule VI of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'may consider consulting' weakens the obligation to consult on press releases, making it optional. Furthermore, 'some proposed press releases' introduces ambiguity regarding which announcements require consultation. Changing 'they have issued' to 'they may have issued' introduces ambiguity as to whether the joint press release was actually issued. Lastly, 'a form similar to' introduces ambiguity regarding whether the press release was the official one.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts SEC regulations concerning full and fair disclosure of material information to investors. Rule 405 defines key terms related to securities offerings. Specifically, if the agreement involves information that would be material to investors, the ambiguity surrounding the press releases could violate the spirit and letter of securities laws, particularly those requiring accurate and timely disclosure of information that could affect investment decisions. Making the consultation and the acknowledgement of a prior release optional weakens the obligation for transparency, potentially misleading investors if the press release contains material information.

perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces ambiguity regarding the commitment to transparency and accurate disclosure by softening the obligation to consult on press releases and acknowledge prior releases. The original text mandated consultation ('shall consult'), which aligns with the principle of full and fair disclosure. The changed text weakens this to 'may consider consulting,' making consultation optional. Additionally, the change from 'have issued' to 'may have issued' creates doubt about whether a joint press release was even issued, which is a move away from transparency and a clear violation. Rule 405 from snippet 1 defines key terms and underscores the need for accurate disclosure, a need that is undermined by the change in the contract. The changes would fail legal scrutiny because they remove accountability.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall maintain, at its own cost, comprehensive product liability insurance, general commercial liability insurance and business interruption insurance at a level which is reasonable and customary taking into account the nature of the Product but which shall have combined limits of not less than $[**] per occurrence. Such insurance shall be with a reputable insurance company and where reasonably possible (taking into account the availability of such insurance) shall be maintained for not less than [**] ([**]) years following the expiry or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should consider maintaining, at its own cost, some product liability insurance, general commercial liability insurance and business interruption insurance at a level they deem appropriate, but which could have combined limits of not less than $[**] per occurrence. Such insurance may be with what they consider a sufficient insurance company and could be maintained for some years following the expiry or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to maintain insurance by replacing 'shall maintain' with 'should consider maintaining', making insurance coverage discretionary. It replaces 'comprehensive' with 'some', and 'reputable' with 'sufficient' giving the party more leeway and less obligations. By replacing the set year amount with 'some years' creates a vague timeline.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 10, Section 2695.4
perturbation.law_explanation: This change directly contradicts California Insurance Code regulations, particularly Title 10, Section 2695.4, which addresses standards for prompt, fair, and equitable settlements of claims. By weakening the requirement for 'comprehensive' and 'reputable' insurance, the modified text allows for inadequate coverage, potentially hindering the ability to promptly and fairly settle claims. This can lead to delayed or insufficient compensation to claimants, violating the core principles outlined in the regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intention of ensuring adequate insurance coverage, which could hinder the ability to promptly and fairly settle claims. By changing 'shall maintain' to 'should consider maintaining', and 'comprehensive' to 'some', it weakens the obligation to have adequate insurance coverage. The scraped snippets did not help support this determination as it was a document not found.
